chunk_summary: Management of complicated measles in SAM includes infection control, empiric antibiotics, cautious rehydration, and age-specific high-dose vitamin A supplementation to prevent complications like corneal injury and pneumonia.
learning_objectives: Understand the risks and complications of measles in children with severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Learn the appropriate use of empiric antibiotics and anti-staphylococcal coverage in complicated measles cases.
Apply age-specific high-dose vitamin A supplementation for measles management in SAM.
Recognize the importance of infection control and follow-up immunization planning post-discharge.
key_facts: Malnourished children are at higher risk of severe measles complications, including pneumonia, diarrhea, and corneal injury.
Vitamin A dosing: 50,000 IU (<6 months), 100,000 IU (6–12 months), 200,000 IU (>12 months).
Avoid routine IV boluses for dehydration unless shock criteria are met; use malnutrition-appropriate oral/NG rehydration.
Empiric antibiotics are essential in complicated SAM with suspected bacterial superinfection.
Anti-staphylococcal coverage is critical in post-measles pneumonia or extensive skin infections.
Infection control measures (isolation, hand hygiene) and catch-up immunization planning are key discharge tasks.
reference_quote: Malnourished children have higher risk of severe or complicated measles, including pneumonia, diarrhea with dehydration, and corneal injury. (WHO, Pocket book of hospital care for children: Severe acute malnutrition chapter)
